coredata.dc:title: Confinement effect of network-structured carbon dots/cellulose nanofiber/magnesium hydroxide for enhanced heavy metal ions capture and immobilization
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Wei-Ming
Wang, Yan
Sun, Yuan
Zhao, Nian-Dan
Wang, Chen
Chen, Zhijun
Guo, Yuan-Ru
Li, Shujun
Pan, Qing-Jiang
coredata.dc:description: To solve pollution problem of heavy metal ions (HMIs) and recover them for sustainable development, a high-efficient-sewage treatment agent, carbon dots/cellulose nanofiber/Mg(OH)2 (CCMg), has been fabricated via a simple hydrothermal method. A variety of characterizations show that cellulose nanofiber (CNF) formed a layered-net structure. Hexagonal Mg(OH)2 flakes of about 100 nm has been attached on CNF. Carbon dots (CDs) around 10–20 nm in size were produced from CNF and distributed along CNF. The extraordinary structural feature endows CCMg with high removal performance towards HMIs. The up-taken capacities reach 992.8 and 667.3 mg g−1 for Cd2+ and Cu2+, respectively. The composite bears excellent durability in treating wastewater. Notably, the qualification of the drinking water can be satisfied while applying CCMg to handle Cu2+ wastewater. The mechanism of removal process has been proposed. Practically, Cd2+/Cu2+ ions were immobilized by CNF due to the space confinement effect. It achieves the facile separation and recovery of HMIs from the sewage, and more importantly, eliminates the risk of secondary contamination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose nanofiber
Magnesium hydroxide
Cadmium and copper ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon dots/cellulose nanofiber/Mg(OH)2 (CCMg) composites
Structure and morphology of CCMg composites
Characterizations of recovered samples and removal mechanism

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Reformation and Development of Local Universities Supported By The Central Government

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (22276046 and U2167219). The Fund of the Reformation and Development of Local Universities Supported By The Central Government (2021) is acknowledged.

This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22276046 and U2167219 ). The Fund of the Reformation and Development of Local Universities Supported By The Central Government (2021) is acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic route of the ternary composite CDs/CNF/Mg(OH)2 (CCMg), along with the immobilizing HMIs process by the dialysis bag that contains CCMg.
XRD patterns (a) and FT-IR spectra (b) of CCMg samples and Mg(OH)2, along with Mg 1s XPS spectrum (c), TG/DTG curves (d), SEM images (e and f) and EDS mapping (g) of CCMg-12.
TEM (a) and HRTEM images (b) of CCMg-12, along with photographs of samples illuminated by 365 nm UV lamp and bright field (c).

The removal capacities of CCMg samples: (a) the removal capacities of various CCMg, (b) the adsorption kinetic curves of CCMg-12 to Cd2+ and Cu2+, (c) pseudo-second-order fitting plots, (d and e) removal isotherms with fitting plots by Langmuir/Freundlich models, (f) the durability test, and (g) the removal efficiencies of CCMg for Cu2+/Cd2+ co-existed solution, along with the removal efficiency versus Cd2+ (h) and Cu2+ (i) of immobilizing HMIs by the dialysis bag that contains CCMg.
XRD patterns (a) and XPS spectra (b) of CCMg-M (M = Cd and Cu), along with SEM images of CCMg-Cd (c, d) and EDS mapping pictures (e).
Removal capacities for Cd2+ and Cu2+ by various reported adsorbents.

head.ce:title: Confinement effect of network-structured carbon dots/cellulose nanofiber/magnesium hydroxide for enhanced heavy metal ions capture and immobilization

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: W. -M. Yin, Y. Wang and Y. Sun contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To solve pollution problem of heavy metal ions (HMIs) and recover them for sustainable development, a high-efficient-sewage treatment agent, carbon dots/cellulose nanofiber/Mg(OH)2 (CCMg), has been fabricated via a simple hydrothermal method. A variety of characterizations show that cellulose nanofiber (CNF) formed a layered-net structure. Hexagonal Mg(OH)2 flakes of about 100 nm has been attached on CNF. Carbon dots (CDs) around 10–20 nm in size were produced from CNF and distributed along CNF. The extraordinary structural feature endows CCMg with high removal performance towards HMIs. The up-taken capacities reach 992.8 and 667.3 mg g−1 for Cd2+ and Cu2+, respectively. The composite bears excellent durability in treating wastewater. Notably, the qualification of the drinking water can be satisfied while applying CCMg to handle Cu2+ wastewater. The mechanism of removal process has been proposed. Practically, Cd2+/Cu2+ ions were immobilized by CNF due to the space confinement effect. It achieves the facile separation and recovery of HMIs from the sewage, and more importantly, eliminates the risk of secondary contamination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous and rapid growth of industrialization, the environment pollutions had attracted global attention due to their serious damage to ecosystem [1–3], especially heavy metal ions (HMIs) pollutants. When HMIs (e.g., Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+ and Cu2+) are discharged into the environment, they may accumulate in human body via the food chain. This effect would damage organs of human beings and cause serious health problem [4–7]. Thus, HMIs pollution becomes one of the most urgent problems for scientists to deal with. In this respect, various approaches have been developed to separate HMIs from the water for different circumstances. For example, methods such as chemical precipitation, immobilization and electrolysis are usually used to handle high-concentration HMIs-contained water in factory or emergency [8–12]; and ions exchange [13], adsorption [14–17] and membrane separation technologies [18] are applied to treat trace HMIs-contained water. However, a method that satisfies to deal with both industrial sewage (high concentration) and drinking water (low) remains rare. Moreover, how to completely separate the recovered HMIs from water is still an open issue to be conquered.

Cellulose is the most abundant carbohydrate biomass material in the world, which is composed of d-glucopyranose units [19–21]. Cellulose nanofiber (CNF) is a kind of cellulose with a diameter of less than 100 nm and a length of several nanometers to several micrometers [22–24]. It has many advantages such as high biodegradability, mechanical flexibility, low density, high mechanical strength as well as environmental friendliness. It was reported that CNFs could be integrated with other performance-enhancing materials to obtain functionalized composites [25–28].

As a kind of nano-scale carbon material, carbon dots (CDs) have become promising for their excellent properties. Various starting materials, especially biomaterials have been explored to prepare CDs. Due to their small size effect, abundant surface functional groups and good adsorption property, CDs have a wide range of applications, such as sewage treatment agent and photoelectric device [29–32]. So far, the preparation condition of CDs with the cellulose biomaterial has mainly been limited to the acidolysis along with high temperature above 180 °C. However, basic condition and relatively low temperature is still underexplored.

As a weak base, magnesium hydroxide is environment-friendly and harmless to human beings. It has been widely used as flame-retardant, ceramics material and food additive [33–36]. In treating polluted water, Mg(OH)2 can work as a mild base and precipitate HMIs by slowly dissolving and releasing of OH−. Up to date, there have been some reports about the use of Mg(OH)2 for HMIs treatment [10,11,15,37].

In this work, we have prepared a ternary composite CDs/CNF/Mg(OH)2 (marked as CCMg) via a simple hydrothermal method at a relatively low temperature (100 °C). The CDs were accessed along with the CNF. The merits of network structure of CNF, excellent adsorption property of CDs and high removal capacity Mg(OH)2 would endow the prepared composite with remarkable Cd2+/Cu2+ HMIs removal performance. The treated water meets the effluent discharged standard for Cd2+ and satisfies the drinkable water standard for Cu2+. The confinement effect of the CNF network was addressed as well as HMIs removal mechanism. The prepared composite made the separation and recovery operations easy and offered a wide application as water treatment agent.

The chemicals used in this experiment were not re-purified and used directly. Degreased cotton was used as the source of cellulose. H2SO4, MgSO4·7H2O and NaOH were purchased from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Company. Cd(NO3)2·4H2O and CuSO4·5H2O were bought from Aladdin Co. Ltd.

1.0 g degreased cotton was soaked in 20 mL 64 % H2SO4. Then the mixture was stirred for 2 h in the water bath at 45 °C. The CNF solution was washed to pH = 4 by centrifugation. After centrifugation, 50 mL deionized water was added into the sediment, and the CNF suspension was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots/cellulose nanofiber/Mg(OH)2 (CCMg) composites
ce:section.ce:para: 3.0 g MgSO4·7H2O was added to the CNF suspension, and adjusted the solution to the required pH = 10, 11 and 12 with 5 mol·L−1 NaOH. After stirring for 30 min, the reaction mixture was transferred to the Teflon reactors. Hydrothermal process was carried out for 4 h at 100 °C. After cooling down, the product was separated via filtration and washing. Final CDs/CNF/Mg(OH)2 (CCMg) composite was obtained via drying. The products were named CCMg−10, −11 and − 12 according to the pH value of the reaction solution.

The crystallinity of the sample was characterized by X-ray diffractometer (D/max-RC, Rigaku, Japan), with Cu Kα radiation (K = 0.15418 nm) and the scanning range is 10–80° (2θ). The morphology and structure were studied by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, S = 4800, Hitachi, Chiyoda-Ku, Japan). The thermal stability was determined by thermogravimetric analyzer (Q50, TA, USA) with heating rate of 10 °C·min−1. Infrared spectra were determined by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, PerkinElmer, USA). Elemental analysis was carried out by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (ESCALAB, Thermo, USA) analysis method. Fluorescence spectrometer (Fluorescence Spectrometer, LS 55, PerkinElmer, USA) was used to characterize the fluorescence performance of samples.

In a typical experiment, 0.1 g CCMg was added into 400 mL HMIs (Cd2+ and Cu2+) solution. The mixture was stirring for 48 h at room temperature. In the certain time, 4 mL supernatant was sampled and tested with atomic absorption spectrometry (PinAAcle 900 T, PerkinElmer, USA) and ICP-MS (PerkinElmer NEXION 350D, USA).

The application experiments were carried out at room temperature. Simulated sewage is 1000 mL 10 mg·L−1 Cd2+/Cu2+ solution. To purify the water, 1.0 g CCMg-12 was put in a dialysis bag (MW = 1000) and then the dialysis bag was immersed in the simulation sewage. After keeping in sewage for 3 h, the dialysis bag was taken out and put into a new simulation sewage to start a new turn to purify water. At the end of each turn, the treated sewage was sampled and then concentrations of HMIs were tested.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology of CCMg composites
ce:section.ce:para: XRD has been applied to explore crystalline structures of CCMg samples. CCMg-10, CCMg-11 and CCMg-12 refer to samples which were prepared at pH of 10, 11, and 12, respectively. It can be seen that all the samples show similar XRD patterns in Fig. 2a. Most of them match the brucite phase of Mg(OH)2 (JCPDS# 44-1482). Moreover, the CCMg composites have additional characteristic peaks at 14.6°, 16.3° and 22.6°. They have been attributed to cellulose of type I (JCPDS# 50-2241) [38,39]. Intense and sharp peaks of cellulose show that CNF has high crystallization degree. It is calculated to be 72 % according to the equation reported in literature [40]. Since the composite samples have low contents of CDs, the related diffraction peaks of CDs do not appear in XRD plots.

The pH of solution would affect the production and crystallization orientation of Mg(OH)2. One can observe that sharper peaks of the Mg(OH)2 component while being prepared at higher pH condition. It indicates that high content of Mg(OH)2 is produced in the composite. Notably, the intensity of the (101) peak is greatly depressed in the composite samples compared to that in the blank Mg(OH)2. It can be deduced that CNF is likely to form interfacial coupling with this facet of Mg(OH)2. Therefore, the interaction would eventually result in thin flakes of Mg(OH)2 [10].

FT-IR spectra have been further applied to analyze the functional groups of CCMg (Fig. 2b). It can be found that all the characteristic peaks of Mg(OH)2 appear in the IR spectra of CCMg composites. The characteristic peak at 3698 cm−1 is attributed to the (Mg)O-H stretching vibration, which indicates the existence of Mg(OH)2 in the composite. In addition, the adsorption bands at about 3342 and 1104 cm−1 are the vibrations of OH and CO groups in CNF. The vibration of Mg-O(H) has been weakened compared to the bare Mg(OH)2, indicating that -OH in Mg(OH)2 may have the interaction with CNF via hydrogen bonding.

To fully understand the chemical environment of elements in the composite, the XPS spectra were recorded in Figs. S1, S2 and 2c. CCMg-12 shows three peaks of binding energies, which are related to C 1s, O 1s and Mg 1s. This gives the evidence that the composite is composed of the elements of C, O and Mg. To the high-resolution scanning spectrum of C 1s in Fig. 2c, three peaks can be fitted. The binding energies of 284.8, 286.3 and 288.3 eV correspond to CC, CO and CO, respectively. Since there are only CC and CO bonds in CNF, it is deduced that CO groups originate from CDs. This gives the evidence that the third component CDs occurs in the composite. The XPS spectrum of O 1s is also investigated. The peak at the binding energy of 531.3 eV is the lattice oxygen in Mg(OH)2; the one at 532.7 eV is CO/CO in CNF and CDs. In addition, the spectrum of Mg 1s has two peaks located at 1303.2 and 1304.5 eV. This suggests the existence of Mg element in the composite. All these evidences prove that three components, CNF, CDs and Mg(OH)2, exist in the composite.

The thermal stability of CCMg was characterized by thermogravimetry analysis (TG). The TG curve of CCMg-12 has two-step pyrolysis as shown in Fig. 2d. The first stage occurs at 229–321 °C. The highest weight loss rate is around 300 °C according to the DTG plot. This is consistent with the pyrolysis temperature of CNF. The second pyrolysis step happens at 333–384 °C. This pyrolysis is attributed to the de-hydroxylation of Mg(OH)2 as reported in literatures [41–43]. According to the weight loss, the prepared composite CCMg-12 is constituted by 40.1 % CNF and 59.9 % Mg(OH)2. Since CDs are zero-dimensional material, their weight can be ignored.

The SEM images have been taken to study the morphology of CCMg composites (Fig. 2e, f and S3). All CCMg composites show layered morphology in general. It is constructed by CNF, which cross-links with each other and forms the layered structure. When using high resolution SEM to zoom in these layers, one can see that Mg(OH)2 flakes with the size about 100 nm were attached on the CNF layer. Moreover, it is found that the pH value during preparation slightly affects the size of Mg(OH)2 flakes, but determines the contents of Mg(OH)2 on CNF. The higher pH the solution is, the more Mg(OH)2 is observed to anchor onto CNF. However, too high pH value like 14 would make Mg(OH)2 pile up on CNF (Figs. S3e and S3f). Consequently, some Mg(OH)2 flakes cannot interact with CNF. In this manner, it may risk the second pollution, because the recovered HMIs still exist in wastewater and cannot be taken out with CNF as designed. Additionally, the element mapping picture of CCMg-12 is presented in Fig. 2g. It shows that carbon, oxygen and magnesium appear in the composite.

To further investigate the microstructure of composite, TEM analyses have been carried out on CCMg-12 (Fig. 3). It is clear to see that CNFs is about 50–100 nm in diameter. These CNFs display a network structure via cross-linking with each other. Mg(OH)2 flakes about 100 nm distribute in the network and are immobilized by the CNF, which is consistent with the SEM results. Notably, CDs are also observed adjacent to CNF; and their sizes are about 10–20 nm (Fig. 3a). The result further evidences that CDs are produced from CNF during the hydrothermal process. The TEM results prove the existence of three components in the composite: Mg(OH)2, CNF and CNF-derived CDs. The plane distance of 0.236 nm is determined from the lattice fringe, which is attributed to the facet of (101) of Mg(OH)2 (Fig. 3b). The thickness of the Mg(OH)2 flake is measured to be 5 nm. Obviously, ultrathin Mg(OH)2 flakes can be prepared by this method. This may be caused by the interaction between CNF and the Mg(OH)2 (101) facet, which in return restricts the Mg(OH)2 growth. The TEM results agree with the XRD ones.

To study the origin of CDs in the composite, the hydrothermal experiment was carried out only with CNF in the same condition. The resulting sample was named CDs/CNF. Fluorescent spectra of CDs/CNF and CCMg-12 were determined. As seen in Fig. S4, CDs/CNF exhibits fluorescent emissions between 450–550 nm, which are consistent with the intrinsic fluorescences of CDs [44–46]. The generally same emissions are found for CCMg-12. It is evident that CDs appear in the composite, which is anticipated to enrich HMIs and promote removal reaction. The CDs/CNF suspension was also illuminated by UV lamp (365 nm) (Figs. 3c and S5). One can see that CDs/CNF suspensions via a hydrothermal operation display a blue light, indicating that CDs are formed during the reaction process. These give the evidence that some CNF fibrils on the surface can be degraded into CDs in the modest pH condition. It also explains that the formed CDs always adhere to CNF in the composite. Meanwhile, the prepared CCMg possesses the same fluorescent property as CDs, regardless of being in the form of gel (before drying) or CCMg (after drying). This means that CDs in the composite is quite stable.

The removal performance of CCMg composites has been fully studied by Cd2+ and Cu2+ (Fig. 4). It is found that the pH value in the preparation process has impact on the removal capacity of obtained composites (Fig. 4a). CCMg-10, prepared at low pH of 10, has 888.1 mg g−1 removal capacity towards Cd2+. In the cases of increasing pH value, the prepared CCMg-11 and -12 show the larger capacity around 992.0 mg g−1. This may be related to the content of Mg(OH)2 component in the composite, which plays a key role in removing HMIs. More Mg(OH)2 is contained in CCMg, larger removal capacity of HMIs is achieved. The contents of Mg(OH)2 in composite is determined by the pH of preparation solution. Thus, CCMg-10 has lower removal capacity than CCMg-11 and CCMg-12. According to this, high pH value is favored for the preparation of CCMg. However, high pH in the solution also makes Mg(OH)2 grow too fast. Fast growth leads Mg(OH)2 flakes piling up on the surface of CNF. This can be observed in SEM pictures of CCMg prepared at pH 14 (Figs. S3e and S3f). In the case, it is difficult to build the interaction of Mg(OH)2 with CNF. The less interaction between them leads to the less efficiency in immobilizing the recovered Cd2+/Cu2+. Therefore, pH 12 is chosen as the optimal condition for preparing CCMg.

The removal kinetics of CCMg-12 towards HMIs were investigated by treating 500 mg L−1 Cd2+/Cu2+ solution (Fig. 4b). For both ions, removal rates reach equilibrium within 10 h. Comparatively, the removal rate for Cu2+ is a tad faster than for Cd2+: it only takes 30 min to achieve 445.6 mg·g−1 removal capacity when treating Cu2+ solution. As identified below, the recovered HMIs of Cd2+ and Cu2+ are in the forms of CdCO3 and Cu4(SO4)(OH)6, respectively. Since there are more anions of SO4 2− than CO3 2− in solution, the removal rate of Cu2+ would be faster than that of Cd2+. However, the molar mass of Cd2+ is larger than Cu2+. This makes the removal rate of Cd2+ faster than Cu2+ when using mass (per gram of adsorbent) to indicate it. The net result of the two factors is that the removal rate for Cu2+ is a little faster than for Cd2+.

Both kinetic curves of Cd2+ and Cu2+ were fitted by the pseudo-first and -second equations (Figs. S6 and 4c). According to the fitting results of pseudo-second equation, the R2 values are 0.9998 and 0.9999 for Cd2+ and Cu2+, respectively (Table S1). As far as fitted R2 is concerned, the pseudo-second-order equation is more suitable to describe the removal process than the pseudo-first equation. Thus, the removing Cd2+/Cu2+ by CCMg is a chemisorption process. Accordingly, the calculated Q e,cal is 994.3 (Cd2+) and 667.3 (Cu2+) mg g−1, which are comparable to the experimental values. Notably, the Elovich model (R2 = 0.8140/0.8728 for Cd2+/Cu2+) also suits for the simulation of the removal process (Figs. S6). This further suggests that the removal is dominated by a chemical process.

The removal isotherms of Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions of CCMg-12 were fitted by both Langmuir and Freundlich models ( Fig. 4d and e), which give large R2 as seen in Table S2. The calculated maximum theoretical removal capacities are 992.8 (Cd2+) and 667.3 (Cu2+) mg g−1. Compared to previously reported adsorbents collected in Table 1 , CCMg shows not only superior removal performance but also good immobilization towards HMIs.

To study the durability for removing HMIs, 10-cycle experiments have been accomplished by CCMg-12 towards 400 mL Cd2+/Cu2+ solutions (20 mg L−1) (Fig. 4f). The removal efficiency against Cd2+ ion still holds more than 99% after running for 10 cycles; and the removal efficiency for Cu2+ ion maintains above 92 %. This indicates that the composite has good durability when being applied to treat sewage.

To deal with complicated situation during application, the treatment for a Cd2+ and Cu2+ mixed wastewater was carried out. The HMIs concentration of simulation sewage was set as 20 mg L−1, i.e., including 10 mg L−1 Cd2+ and 10 mg L−1 Cu2+. It is found that CCMg-12 still has good removal performance as shown in Fig. 4g. The removal efficiencies reach about 98 % within 1 h and almost 100 % after 3 h for the Cd2+ and Cu2+ co-existed sewage.

In some circumstances, the concentration of HMIs pollutants is quite low in water. To this end, a lab-made system was designed and applied with 1 L Cd2+/Cu2+ simulation sewage (10 mg L−1) in a water tank. A dialysis bag with 1.0 g CCMg-12 in it was immersed in the sewage (Fig. 1 ). Every 3 h, the treated water in the water tank was sampled for testing and then replaced by a new simulate sewage. After renewed for 10 times (10L), the removal efficiency for Cd2+/Cu2+ in sewage still maintains more than 99.8%/99.9% (Fig. 4h and i). The concentrations of remained HMI are listed in Table S3. All of them satisfy the qualification of emission standards. Notably, the concentration of residual Cu2+ ion is even lower than the drinking water standard (< 2 mg·L−1) [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of recovered samples and removal mechanism

ce:section.ce:para: To fully understand the process of removing and immobilizing HMIs, the recovered samples, marked as CCMg-M (M = Cd and Cu) were fully characterized.

Their XRD patterns are presented in Fig. 5a. Cellulose diffraction peaks exist in patterns of both recovered CCMg-Cd and Cu according to the standard card (JCPDS# 50-2241). The phases of CdCO3 and Cu4(SO4)(OH)6 are also recognized. So the recovered Cd2+ and Cu2+ appear in the forms of carbonate (JCPDS# 42-1342) and basic sulphate (JCPDS# 43-0670 and 97-009-7703), respectively.

The XPS spectra of CCMg-Cd/Cu were also measured (Figs. 5b, S7 and S8). The strong peaks belonging to Cd2+ and Cu2+ are found at binding energies of 405.6 and 932.0 eV, respectively. These also evidence that Cd2+/Cu2+ have been recovered from the water and deposited onto CNF fibrils. Despite careful inspection, signals of Mg in XPS spectra of CCMg-Cd/Cu were not found. It implies that Mg2+ is completely exchanged with Cd2+/Cu2+. This helps recover Cd2+/Cu2+ as pure phase once getting rid of cellulose.

By comparing SEM pictures in Fig. 2 with Figs. 5c and S9, one can find changes in the morphology before and after up-taken Cd2+/Cu2+. In the recovered samples, 1D nanowires of CdCO3 with the diameter of about 20 nm are found, which are attaching to CNF (Fig. 5d). To CCMg-Cu, it shows the morphology of particles (Fig. S9). Combined with EDS mappings (Figs. 5e and S10), it is shown that both Cd2+ and Cu2+ have been captured by the CNF network and immobilized on its surface.

It has been identified that the composite CCMg bears a CNF network structure. As the substrate, CNF couples with CDs and Mg(OH)2. Since CDs distribute on surface of CNF fibrils and are negatively charged [52–54], they help to enrich Cd2+/Cu2+ around CNF network and capture them in dialysis bag. Mg(OH)2 in the composite firstly offers to adsorb and then couple Cd2+/Cu2+. Secondly, the Mg(OH)2 component precipitates Cd2+ and Cu2+. It releases OH− to solution and forms a basic microenvironment around CNF fibrils as shown in Eqs. (1)–(5). Then Cd2+ and Cu2+ nearby are deposited onto CNF in forms of CdCO3 and Cu4(SO4)(OH)6, respectively The recovered precipitates chemically interact with CNF, which allows that the removal process only happens around CNF (i.e. in a dialysis bag) other than the whole solution system.

The space confinement effect has been found important in the removal process of HMIs. As characterized, the CNF fibrils constructed a network as confined space. It makes Cd2+/Cu2+ ions freely drift into CNF (in the dialysis bag); subsequently, therein the precipitation reaction happens rapidly and entirely around CNF; and then, the newly-formed precipitates could build interaction with CNF network. Finally, the recovered HMIs are immobilized on CNF. In brief, the confinement effect has made the separation and recovery of HMIs easy and complete. It is also very practical and convenient to eliminate HMIs pollutants just by taking the spent dialysis bag out of the treated water. Moreover, it would avoid the secondary contamination.

In the work, the composite, carbon dots/cellulose nanofiber/Mg(OH)2 (CCMg), was successfully prepared with the goal of trapping heavy metal ions (HMIs) in a high-efficiency way. Cellulose nanofiber (CNF) was obtained by the hydrolysis of cotton fibers. The Mg(OH)2 flakes were dispersed into the CNF network, and the component CDs was anchored onto CNFs. The combination with CNF made an ultra-thin morphology for Mg(OH)2.

The composite CCMg shows good performance in treating HMIs of wastewater. The up-taken capacities reach as high as 992.8 and 667.3 mg g−1 for Cd2+ and Cu2+, respectively. The good durability was proved by the high efficiency kept after 10-cycle operation. While applying 1.0 g CCMg to handle 10 L low-concentration wastewater (10 mg L−1), more than 99.6% removal efficiency was maintained. The removal process and mechanism were revealed. To start the treatment, CDs, CNF as well as oxo parts of Mg(OH)2 would attract Cd2+/Cu2+ by the electrostatic force. The precipitation happened around CNF fibrils. Notably, the space confinement effect greatly enhanced the reaction. Consequently, the recovered CCMg-Cd/Cu would be trapped by the CNF network, which made the separation easy and avoided the secondary contamination.

Wei-Ming Yin: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Yan Wang: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Yuan Sun: Investigation, Formal analysis. Nian-Dan Zhao: Investigation. Chen Wang: Investigation. Zhijun Chen: Investigation. Yuan-Ru Guo: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Shujun Li: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Qing-Jiang Pan: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (22276046 and U2167219). The Fund of the Reformation and Development of Local Universities Supported By The Central Government (2021) is acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2023.124194.
